title: Forest plots for regression results
text:
Forest plots are not limited to meta-analyses. Any table of point estimates with confidence intervals can be displayed as a forest plot — results from regression models, dose-response analyses, multi-outcome studies, and more.
This vignette shows how to go from a fitted R model to a forest plot in a few lines of code using broom::tidy() to extract parameters. broom is not a hard dependency of forrest; it is used here solely as a convenient extraction tool. You can pass any data frame with the right columns directly to forrest().

title: Forest plots for regression results
text:
Simulated data

set.seed(42)
n <- 500

cohort <- data.frame(
  sbp      = 120 + rnorm(n, sd = 15),
  age      = runif(n, 30, 75),
  female   = rbinom(n, 1, 0.5),
  bmi      = rnorm(n, 26, 4),
  smoker   = rbinom(n, 1, 0.2),
  activity = rbinom(n, 1, 0.4)
)
# Introduce real signal
cohort$sbp <- cohort$sbp +
  0.35 * cohort$age -
  5.0  * cohort$female +
  0.4  * cohort$bmi -
  2.0  * cohort$smoker +
  2.0  * cohort$activity +
  rnorm(n, sd = 6)

# Binary outcome (hypertension: SBP ≥ 140)
cohort$htn <- as.integer(cohort$sbp >= 140)

title: Forest plots for regression results
text:
Multiple predictors from one model
The simplest workflow: fit a model, call broom::tidy(conf.int = TRUE), and pass the result to forrest(). Column names estimate, conf.low, and conf.high map directly to the estimate, lower, and upper arguments.

fit_lm <- lm(sbp ~ age + female + bmi + smoker + activity, data = cohort)

coefs <- broom::tidy(fit_lm, conf.int = TRUE)
coefs <- coefs[coefs$term != "(Intercept)", ]

# Tidy labels
coefs$term <- c(
  "Age (per 1 year)",
  "Female sex",
  "BMI (per 1 kg/m²)",
  "Current smoker",
  "Physically active"
)

forrest(
  coefs,
  estimate = "estimate",
  lower    = "conf.low",
  upper    = "conf.high",
  label    = "term",
  header   = "Predictor",
  xlab     = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)",
  stripe   = TRUE
)








For logistic regression, set exponentiate = TRUE in broom::tidy() to get odds ratios directly, then set log_scale = TRUE and ref_line = 1 in forrest():

fit_glm <- glm(htn ~ age + female + bmi + smoker + activity,
               data   = cohort,
               family = binomial)

or_coefs <- broom::tidy(fit_glm, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE)
or_coefs <- or_coefs[or_coefs$term != "(Intercept)", ]
or_coefs$term <- c(
  "Age (per 1 year)",
  "Female sex",
  "BMI (per 1 kg/m²)",
  "Current smoker",
  "Physically active"
)

# Add formatted OR column
or_coefs$or_text <- sprintf(
  "%.2f (%.2f, %.2f)",
  or_coefs$estimate,
  or_coefs$conf.low,
  or_coefs$conf.high
)

forrest(
  or_coefs,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "conf.low",
  upper      = "conf.high",
  label      = "term",
  log_scale  = TRUE,
  ref_line   = 1,
  header     = "Predictor",
  cols       = c("OR (95% CI)" = "or_text"),
  widths     = c(3, 4, 2),
  xlab       = "Odds ratio (95% CI)"
)

title: Forest plots for regression results
text:
Same predictor across adjustment models
A common reporting pattern is to show how an association changes as covariates are progressively added. Extract the row for the exposure of interest from each model and combine.

m1 <- glm(htn ~ age,                          data = cohort, family = binomial)
m2 <- glm(htn ~ age + female,                 data = cohort, family = binomial)
m3 <- glm(htn ~ age + female + bmi + smoker + activity, data = cohort, family = binomial)

# Extract the age row from each model
extract_age <- function(mod, label) {
  row <- broom::tidy(mod, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE)
  row <- row[row$term == "age", ]
  row$model <- label
  row
}

age_across_models <- rbind(
  extract_age(m1, "Model 1: unadjusted"),
  extract_age(m2, "Model 2: + sex"),
  extract_age(m3, "Model 3: + sex, BMI, smoking, activity")
)

forrest(
  age_across_models,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "conf.low",
  upper      = "conf.high",
  label      = "model",
  log_scale  = TRUE,
  ref_line   = 1,
  xlab       = "Odds ratio for age (per 1 year, 95% CI)"
)

title: Forest plots for regression results
text:
Same predictor across multiple outcomes
To compare one exposure against several outcomes, loop over outcomes, extract the exposure row from each model, and stack into a single data frame. Use group to colour by outcome domain.

outcomes <- list(
  list(var = "sbp",    label = "Systolic BP (mmHg)",       domain = "Continuous"),
  list(var = "htn",    label = "Hypertension (binary)",    domain = "Binary"),
  list(var = "bmi",    label = "BMI (kg/m²)",              domain = "Continuous"),
  list(var = "smoker", label = "Current smoker (binary)",  domain = "Binary")
)

rows <- lapply(outcomes, function(o) {
  if (o$domain == "Continuous") {
    fit <- lm(
      as.formula(paste(o$var, "~ activity + age + female")),
      data = cohort
    )
    r <- broom::tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE)
  } else {
    fit <- glm(
      as.formula(paste(o$var, "~ activity + age + female")),
      data   = cohort,
      family = binomial
    )
    r <- broom::tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE)
  }
  r <- r[r$term == "activity", ]
  r$outcome <- o$label
  r$domain  <- o$domain
  r
})
multi_out <- do.call(rbind, rows)

forrest(
  multi_out,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "conf.low",
  upper      = "conf.high",
  label      = "outcome",
  group      = "domain",
  ref_line   = 0,
  legend_pos = "topright",
  xlab       = "Effect of physical activity (coefficient or OR, 95% CI)",
  title      = "Association of physical activity with multiple outcomes"
)









Note: Because continuous and binary outcomes are on different scales, a single reference line may not be appropriate for all rows. Consider displaying them in separate panels, or use ref_line = NULL and add contextual annotations.

title: Forest plots for regression results
text:
Dose-response (exposure categories)
For categorical exposures (e.g., quartiles, tertiles), the reference category has estimate = NA since no CI can be drawn for it. Use a text column to show the reference label.

# Assign quartiles of age
cohort$age_q <- cut(cohort$age, breaks = quantile(cohort$age, 0:4 / 4),
                    include.lowest = TRUE,
                    labels = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"))
cohort$age_q <- relevel(cohort$age_q, ref = "Q1")

fit_q <- glm(htn ~ age_q + female + bmi + smoker + activity,
             data = cohort, family = binomial)
q_coefs <- broom::tidy(fit_q, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE)
q_coefs <- q_coefs[grep("^age_q", q_coefs$term), ]

# Build the full table including the reference row
q_plot <- rbind(
  data.frame(
    term      = "age_qQ1",
    estimate  = NA_real_,
    conf.low  = NA_real_,
    conf.high = NA_real_,
    or_ci     = "1.00 (ref)"
  ),
  data.frame(
    term      = q_coefs$term,
    estimate  = q_coefs$estimate,
    conf.low  = q_coefs$conf.low,
    conf.high = q_coefs$conf.high,
    or_ci     = sprintf("%.2f (%.2f, %.2f)",
                        q_coefs$estimate,
                        q_coefs$conf.low,
                        q_coefs$conf.high)
  )
)
q_plot$label  <- c("Q1 (reference)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4")
q_plot$is_sum <- FALSE

forrest(
  q_plot,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "conf.low",
  upper      = "conf.high",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  log_scale  = TRUE,
  ref_line   = 1,
  header     = "Age quartile",
  cols       = c("OR (95% CI)" = "or_ci"),
  widths     = c(2.5, 4, 2),
  xlab       = "Odds ratio for hypertension (95% CI)"
)

title: Forest plots for regression results
text:
Marginal estimates (g-formula, IPW, etc.)
For causal methods that return marginal effects directly (parametric g-formula, inverse probability weighting, targeted maximum likelihood estimation), the output is typically already a data frame of estimates and confidence intervals. Pass it directly to forrest() — no model extraction step is needed.

# Hypothetical marginal risk differences from a g-formula analysis
gform_dat <- data.frame(
  contrast  = c(
    "Active vs. inactive (overall)",
    "  Age < 50",
    "  Age 50–65",
    "  Age > 65"
  ),
  estimate  = c(-0.068, -0.045, -0.072, -0.089),
  lower     = c(-0.105, -0.091, -0.122, -0.148),
  upper     = c(-0.031,  0.001, -0.022, -0.030),
  is_sum    = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

forrest(
  gform_dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "contrast",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  ref_line   = 0,
  xlab       = "Marginal risk difference (95% CI)",
  title      = "Effect of physical activity on hypertension risk"
)

title: Forest plots for regression results
text:
Saving plots
Export any plot to a file with save_forrest(). The device is inferred from the file extension (.pdf, .png, .svg, .tiff).

save_forrest(
  file   = "regression_forest.pdf",
  plot   = function() {
    forrest(
      coefs,
      estimate = "estimate",
      lower    = "conf.low",
      upper    = "conf.high",
      label    = "term",
      stripe   = TRUE,
      xlab     = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)"
    )
  },
  width  = 9,
  height = 5
)

title: Citation
text:
Citation
To cite package ‘forrest’ in publications use:

Fabbri L (2026). forrest: Publication-Ready Forest Plots. R package version 0.2.0, https://github.com/lorenzoFabbri/forrest.

title: forrest
text:
Draws a publication-ready forest plot from a data frame. Each row represents one estimate — a study, a predictor, a model, a subgroup, or any other unit of analysis. Rows whose estimate is NA are treated as spacer/header rows: they produce no point or confidence interval, and their label is rendered in bold if non-empty.



forrest(
  data,
  estimate,
  lower,
  upper,
  label = NULL,
  group = NULL,
  is_summary = NULL,
  weight = NULL,
  ref_line = 0,
  log_scale = FALSE,
  xlim = NULL,
  xlab = "Estimate (95% CI)",
  title = NULL,
  header = NULL,
  cols = NULL,
  widths = NULL,
  stripe = FALSE,
  pch = 15,
  lwd = 2,
  cex = 1,
  col = NULL,
  legend_pos = "topright",
  ...
)







data


A data frame, tibble, or data.table.




estimate


Column name (string) for point estimates.




lower


Column name (string) for lower confidence interval bounds.




upper


Column name (string) for upper confidence interval bounds.




label


Column name (string) for row labels displayed on the y-axis or in the left text panel. If NULL, row numbers are used.




group


Column name (string) for a grouping variable. Rows that share a group value receive the same colour, and a legend is drawn automatically.




is_summary


Column name (string) of a logical vector. Rows where this is TRUE are drawn as filled diamonds (e.g. pooled or overall estimates) rather than squares with whiskers.




weight


Column name (string) of numeric row weights. When provided, point size scales as cex * sqrt(weight / max(weight)), so rows with larger weights appear with a bigger marker. Weights for summary rows are ignored (diamond size is fixed by cex).




ref_line


Numeric. Position of the vertical reference line (e.g. 0 for differences, 1 for ratio measures on the natural scale). Set to NULL to suppress. Default is 0.




log_scale


Logical. If TRUE, apply a log transformation to the x-axis. Useful when plotting odds ratios, hazard ratios, or risk ratios on the natural scale. Default is FALSE.




xlim


Numeric vector of length 2 giving x-axis limits. Computed from the data when NULL (default). Confidence intervals that extend beyond xlim are clipped at the axis boundary and an arrow is drawn to indicate truncation.




xlab


Label for the x-axis. Default is “Estimate (95% CI)”.




title


Plot title. Default is NULL (no title).




header


Optional header string placed above the label column. When cols is provided this appears above the left text panel; otherwise it is drawn above the topmost row on the y-axis.




cols


Named character vector specifying extra text columns to display to the right of the plot. Names become column headers; values are column names in data. Example: cols = c(“OR (95% CI)” = “or_text”, “P” = “pval_text”).




widths


Numeric vector of relative panel widths. When cols is NULL, ignored. Otherwise, length must equal 2 + length(cols): label panel, plot panel, then one entry per extra column. Sensible defaults are chosen automatically.




stripe


Logical. If TRUE, alternate rows are shaded with a light grey background to improve readability. Default is FALSE.




pch


Point character for non-summary rows. Default is 15 (filled square), which is conventional for forest plots.




lwd


Line width for confidence interval whiskers. Default is 2.




cex


Point size multiplier. Default is 1.




col


Colour or character vector of colours. When NULL (default) and group is specified, the Okabe-Ito colorblind-safe palette is used. When NULL and no group, a single dark colour is used.




legend_pos


Position of the legend when group is supplied. Passed to legend(). Set to NULL to suppress the legend. Default is “topright”.




…


Additional graphical parameters passed to the internal tinyplot::tinyplot() call that sets up the main plot area (e.g. cex.axis, cex.lab, font.main).






Invisibly returns NULL. Called for its side effect of producing a plot.




library("forrest")

# Basic forest plot — results from a single regression model
dat <- data.frame(
  label    = c("Exposure A", "Exposure B", "Exposure C", "Exposure D"),
  estimate = c(0.42, -0.18, 0.31, -0.07),
  lower    = c(0.22, -0.38, 0.12, -0.24),
  upper    = c(0.62,  0.02, 0.50,  0.10)
)
forrest(dat,
  estimate = "estimate",
  lower    = "lower",
  upper    = "upper",
  label    = "label",
  xlab     = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)"
)







# Multiple models with subgroup headers and a text column
dat2 <- data.frame(
  label    = c(
    "Model 1", "  Exposure A", "  Exposure B",
    "", "Model 2", "  Exposure A", "  Exposure B"
  ),
  estimate = c(NA,  0.42, -0.18, NA, NA,  0.35, -0.14),
  lower    = c(NA,  0.22, -0.38, NA, NA,  0.16, -0.32),
  upper    = c(NA,  0.62,  0.02, NA, NA,  0.54,  0.04),
  is_sum   = rep(FALSE, 7),
  est_ci   = c(
    "", "0.42 (0.22, 0.62)", "-0.18 (-0.38, 0.02)",
    "", "", "0.35 (0.16, 0.54)", "-0.14 (-0.32, 0.04)"
  )
)
forrest(dat2,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  header     = "Predictor",
  cols       = c("Coef (95% CI)" = "est_ci")
)

title: forrest
text:
Draws a publication-ready forest plot from a data frame. Each row represents one estimate — a study, a predictor, a model, a subgroup, or any other unit of analysis. Rows whose estimate is NA are treated as spacer/header rows: they produce no point or confidence interval, and their label is rendered in bold if non-empty.



forrest(
  data,
  estimate,
  lower,
  upper,
  label = NULL,
  group = NULL,
  is_summary = NULL,
  weight = NULL,
  ref_line = 0,
  log_scale = FALSE,
  xlim = NULL,
  xlab = "Estimate (95% CI)",
  title = NULL,
  header = NULL,
  cols = NULL,
  widths = NULL,
  stripe = FALSE,
  pch = 15,
  lwd = 2,
  cex = 1,
  col = NULL,
  legend_pos = "topright",
  ...
)







data


A data frame, tibble, or data.table.

Column name (string) for point estimates.

Column name (string) for lower confidence interval bounds.




upper


Column name (string) for upper confidence interval bounds.

Column name (string) for row labels displayed on the y-axis or in the left text panel. If NULL, row numbers are used.

Column name (string) for a grouping variable. Rows that share a group value receive the same colour, and a legend is drawn automatically.




is_summary


Column name (string) of a logical vector. Rows where this is TRUE are drawn as filled diamonds (e.g. pooled or overall estimates) rather than squares with whiskers.




weight


Column name (string) of numeric row weights. When provided, point size scales as cex * sqrt(weight / max(weight)), so rows with larger weights appear with a bigger marker. Weights for summary rows are ignored (diamond size is fixed by cex).




ref_line


Numeric. Position of the vertical reference line (e.g. 0 for differences, 1 for ratio measures on the natural scale). Set to NULL to suppress. Default is 0.




log_scale


Logical. If TRUE, apply a log transformation to the x-axis. Useful when plotting odds ratios, hazard ratios, or risk ratios on the natural scale. Default is FALSE.




xlim


Numeric vector of length 2 giving x-axis limits. Computed from the data when NULL (default). Confidence intervals that extend beyond xlim are clipped at the axis boundary and an arrow is drawn to indicate truncation.




xlab


Label for the x-axis. Default is “Estimate (95% CI)”.

Plot title. Default is NULL (no title).




header


Optional header string placed above the label column. When cols is provided this appears above the left text panel; otherwise it is drawn above the topmost row on the y-axis.

Named character vector specifying extra text columns to display to the right of the plot. Names become column headers; values are column names in data. Example: cols = c(“OR (95% CI)” = “or_text”, “P” = “pval_text”).




widths


Numeric vector of relative panel widths. When cols is NULL, ignored. Otherwise, length must equal 2 + length(cols): label panel, plot panel, then one entry per extra column. Sensible defaults are chosen automatically.

Logical. If TRUE, alternate rows are shaded with a light grey background to improve readability. Default is FALSE.




pch


Point character for non-summary rows. Default is 15 (filled square), which is conventional for forest plots.




lwd


Line width for confidence interval whiskers. Default is 2.

Point size multiplier. Default is 1.




col


Colour or character vector of colours. When NULL (default) and group is specified, the Okabe-Ito colorblind-safe palette is used. When NULL and no group, a single dark colour is used.




legend_pos


Position of the legend when group is supplied. Passed to legend(). Set to NULL to suppress the legend. Default is “topright”.




…


Additional graphical parameters passed to the internal tinyplot::tinyplot() call that sets up the main plot area (e.g. cex.axis, cex.lab, font.main).






Invisibly returns NULL. Called for its side effect of producing a plot.




library("forrest")

# Basic forest plot — results from a single regression model
dat <- data.frame(
  label    = c("Exposure A", "Exposure B", "Exposure C", "Exposure D"),
  estimate = c(0.42, -0.18, 0.31, -0.07),
  lower    = c(0.22, -0.38, 0.12, -0.24),
  upper    = c(0.62,  0.02, 0.50,  0.10)
)
forrest(dat,
  estimate = "estimate",
  lower    = "lower",
  upper    = "upper",
  label    = "label",
  xlab     = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)"
)







# Multiple models with subgroup headers and a text column
dat2 <- data.frame(
  label    = c(
    "Model 1", "  Exposure A", "  Exposure B",
    "", "Model 2", "  Exposure A", "  Exposure B"
  ),
  estimate = c(NA,  0.42, -0.18, NA, NA,  0.35, -0.14),
  lower    = c(NA,  0.22, -0.38, NA, NA,  0.16, -0.32),
  upper    = c(NA,  0.62,  0.02, NA, NA,  0.54,  0.04),
  is_sum   = rep(FALSE, 7),
  est_ci   = c(
    "", "0.42 (0.22, 0.62)", "-0.18 (-0.38, 0.02)",
    "", "", "0.35 (0.16, 0.54)", "-0.14 (-0.32, 0.04)"
  )
)
forrest(dat2,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  header     = "Predictor",
  cols       = c("Coef (95% CI)" = "est_ci")
)

title: forrest 0.2.0
text:
save_forrest() — new exported function to write a forest plot to a file. The graphics device (PDF, PNG, SVG, TIFF) is inferred from the file extension; resolution can be controlled via dpi for raster formats.
forrest() gains a stripe argument (FALSE by default). When TRUE, alternate rows are shaded with a light-grey background to improve readability in tables with many rows.





Revised framing: forrest is now documented as a general-purpose tool for any tabular estimates-and-CIs data, not only meta-analyses. The description, README, and vignettes have been updated accordingly.
New vignette Forest plots for regression results covers four practical patterns: multiple predictors from one model, comparing estimates across adjustment models, same predictor across multiple outcomes, and dose-response (exposure categories). Examples use broom::tidy() for parameter extraction.
Getting-started vignette updated with a stripe example and a Saving plots section.
README now leads with a regression-model example and an updated feature comparison table.





Drawing helpers (draw_diamond(), draw_text_panel()) moved from R/utils.R to a dedicated R/draw.R, improving code organisation.
save_forrest() lives in its own R/save.R.





Horizontal gridlines are now drawn at lwd = 0.7 (was 0.8) for a slightly lighter appearance.

title: forrest 0.2.0
text:
save_forrest() — new exported function to write a forest plot to a file. The graphics device (PDF, PNG, SVG, TIFF) is inferred from the file extension; resolution can be controlled via dpi for raster formats.
forrest() gains a stripe argument (FALSE by default). When TRUE, alternate rows are shaded with a light-grey background to improve readability in tables with many rows.

title: forrest 0.2.0
text:
Revised framing: forrest is now documented as a general-purpose tool for any tabular estimates-and-CIs data, not only meta-analyses. The description, README, and vignettes have been updated accordingly.
New vignette Forest plots for regression results covers four practical patterns: multiple predictors from one model, comparing estimates across adjustment models, same predictor across multiple outcomes, and dose-response (exposure categories). Examples use broom::tidy() for parameter extraction.
Getting-started vignette updated with a stripe example and a Saving plots section.
README now leads with a regression-model example and an updated feature comparison table.

title: forrest 0.2.0
text:
Drawing helpers (draw_diamond(), draw_text_panel()) moved from R/utils.R to a dedicated R/draw.R, improving code organisation.
save_forrest() lives in its own R/save.R.

title: forrest 0.2.0
text: Horizontal gridlines are now drawn at lwd = 0.7 (was 0.8) for a slightly lighter appearance.

title: forrest
text:
Writes the plot produced by forrest() (or any base-R plotting code) to a file. The graphics device is inferred from the file extension.



save_forrest(file, plot, width = 7, height = 5, dpi = 300, bg = "white")







file


Output file path. Supported extensions: .pdf, .png, .svg, .tiff.




plot


A zero-argument function (or function() { … } block) whose body calls forrest(). It is evaluated inside the open graphics device.




width


Plot width in inches. Default 7.




height


Plot height in inches. Default 5.




dpi


Resolution in dots per inch for raster formats (.png, .tiff). Ignored for vector formats (.pdf, .svg). Default 300.




bg


Background colour. Default “white”.






Invisibly returns file.




library("forrest")

dat <- data.frame(
  label    = c("Exposure A", "Exposure B", "Exposure C"),
  estimate = c(0.42, -0.18, 0.31),
  lower    = c(0.22, -0.38, 0.12),
  upper    = c(0.62,  0.02, 0.50)
)
tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".pdf")
save_forrest(tmp, function() {
  forrest(
    dat,
    estimate = "estimate",
    lower    = "lower",
    upper    = "upper",
    label    = "label",
    xlab     = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)"
  )
})

title: forrest
text:
Writes the plot produced by forrest() (or any base-R plotting code) to a file. The graphics device is inferred from the file extension.



save_forrest(file, plot, width = 7, height = 5, dpi = 300, bg = "white")







file


Output file path. Supported extensions: .pdf, .png, .svg, .tiff.




plot


A zero-argument function (or function() { … } block) whose body calls forrest(). It is evaluated inside the open graphics device.




width


Plot width in inches. Default 7.




height


Plot height in inches. Default 5.




dpi


Resolution in dots per inch for raster formats (.png, .tiff). Ignored for vector formats (.pdf, .svg). Default 300.




bg


Background colour. Default “white”.






Invisibly returns file.




library("forrest")

dat <- data.frame(
  label    = c("Exposure A", "Exposure B", "Exposure C"),
  estimate = c(0.42, -0.18, 0.31),
  lower    = c(0.22, -0.38, 0.12),
  upper    = c(0.62,  0.02, 0.50)
)
tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".pdf")
save_forrest(tmp, function() {
  forrest(
    dat,
    estimate = "estimate",
    lower    = "lower",
    upper    = "upper",
    label    = "label",
    xlab     = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)"
  )
})

title: forrest
text:
forrest creates publication-ready forest plots from any data frame that contains point estimates and confidence intervals — regression models, subgroup analyses, meta-analyses, dose-response patterns, and more. A single dependency (tinyplot) keeps the footprint minimal.
Key features:

Works with any estimates and CIs: regression coefficients, ORs, HRs, MDs, …
Group and subgroup headings via NA estimate rows
Summary estimates rendered as filled diamonds
Alternating row stripes for readability (stripe = TRUE)
Group colouring with automatic Okabe-Ito legend
Optional text columns (formatted estimates, p-values, …) alongside the plot
Log-scale x-axis for ratio measures
CI clipping at axis limits with directional arrows
Point size proportional to row weights
Export to PDF, PNG, SVG, or TIFF with save_forrest()
Works with data.frame, tibble, and data.table



# Development version from GitHub:
# install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("lorenzoFabbri/forrest")





Use broom::tidy() to extract parameters from a fitted model and pass the result directly to forrest().
library(forrest)

set.seed(1)
n   <- 300
dat <- data.frame(
  sbp    = 120 + rnorm(n, sd = 15),
  age    = runif(n, 30, 70),
  female = rbinom(n, 1, 0.5),
  bmi    = rnorm(n, 26, 4),
  smoker = rbinom(n, 1, 0.2)
)
dat$sbp <- dat$sbp + 0.3 * dat$age - 4 * dat$female +
           0.5 * dat$bmi - 2 * dat$smoker + rnorm(n, sd = 6)

fit   <- lm(sbp ~ age + female + bmi + smoker, data = dat)
coefs <- broom::tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE)
coefs <- coefs[coefs$term != "(Intercept)", ]
coefs$term <- c("Age (per 1 y)", "Female sex",
                "BMI (per 1 kg/m²)", "Current smoker")

forrest(
  coefs,
  estimate = "estimate",
  lower    = "conf.low",
  upper    = "conf.high",
  label    = "term",
  xlab     = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)",
  stripe   = TRUE
)




sub <- data.frame(
  label  = c(
    "Sex", "  Female", "  Male", "  Overall",
    "", "Region", "  North", "  South", "  Overall"
  ),
  est    = c(NA, -0.10, -0.05, -0.07, NA, NA, -0.12, -0.02, -0.07),
  lo     = c(NA, -0.22, -0.18, -0.16, NA, NA, -0.28, -0.12, -0.16),
  hi     = c(NA,  0.02,  0.08,  0.02, NA, NA,  0.04,  0.08,  0.02),
  is_sum = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE),
  text   = c(
    "", "-0.10 (-0.22, 0.02)", "-0.05 (-0.18, 0.08)",
    "-0.07 (-0.16, 0.02)", "",
    "", "-0.12 (-0.28, 0.04)", "-0.02 (-0.12, 0.08)",
    "-0.07 (-0.16, 0.02)"
  )
)

forrest(
  sub,
  estimate   = "est",
  lower      = "lo",
  upper      = "hi",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  header     = "Subgroup",
  cols       = c("Estimate (95% CI)" = "text"),
  widths     = c(2.2, 4, 2.5)
)




or_dat <- data.frame(
  study  = c("Adams 2016", "Baker 2018", "Chen 2019", "Pooled"),
  est    = c(1.45, 0.88, 1.21, 1.09),
  lo     = c(1.10, 0.65, 0.92, 0.94),
  hi     = c(1.91, 1.19, 1.59, 1.26),
  is_sum = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

forrest(
  or_dat,
  estimate   = "est",
  lower      = "lo",
  upper      = "hi",
  label      = "study",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  log_scale  = TRUE,
  ref_line   = 1,
  xlab       = "Odds ratio (95% CI)"
)








Feature
forrest
forestplot
forestploter
ggforestplot

title: forrest
text:
# Development version from GitHub:
# install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("lorenzoFabbri/forrest")

title: forrest
text:
Use broom::tidy() to extract parameters from a fitted model and pass the result directly to forrest().
library(forrest)

set.seed(1)
n   <- 300
dat <- data.frame(
  sbp    = 120 + rnorm(n, sd = 15),
  age    = runif(n, 30, 70),
  female = rbinom(n, 1, 0.5),
  bmi    = rnorm(n, 26, 4),
  smoker = rbinom(n, 1, 0.2)
)
dat$sbp <- dat$sbp + 0.3 * dat$age - 4 * dat$female +
           0.5 * dat$bmi - 2 * dat$smoker + rnorm(n, sd = 6)

fit   <- lm(sbp ~ age + female + bmi + smoker, data = dat)
coefs <- broom::tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE)
coefs <- coefs[coefs$term != "(Intercept)", ]
coefs$term <- c("Age (per 1 y)", "Female sex",
                "BMI (per 1 kg/m²)", "Current smoker")

forrest(
  coefs,
  estimate = "estimate",
  lower    = "conf.low",
  upper    = "conf.high",
  label    = "term",
  xlab     = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)",
  stripe   = TRUE
)




sub <- data.frame(
  label  = c(
    "Sex", "  Female", "  Male", "  Overall",
    "", "Region", "  North", "  South", "  Overall"
  ),
  est    = c(NA, -0.10, -0.05, -0.07, NA, NA, -0.12, -0.02, -0.07),

  is_sum = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE),

    "", "-0.10 (-0.22, 0.02)", "-0.05 (-0.18, 0.08)",
    "-0.07 (-0.16, 0.02)", "",
    "", "-0.12 (-0.28, 0.04)", "-0.02 (-0.12, 0.08)",
    "-0.07 (-0.16, 0.02)"
  )
)

forrest(
  sub,
  estimate   = "est",
  lower      = "lo",
  upper      = "hi",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  header     = "Subgroup",
  cols       = c("Estimate (95% CI)" = "text"),
  widths     = c(2.2, 4, 2.5)

or_dat <- data.frame(

  est    = c(1.45, 0.88, 1.21, 1.09),

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
forrest creates publication-ready forest plots from any data frame that contains point estimates and confidence intervals. A single function, forrest(), handles the full range of use cases — regression model results, subgroup analyses, meta-analyses, dose-response patterns, and more.
The only hard dependency is tinyplot. forrest works with base R data frames, tibbles, and data.tables.

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Basic forest plot
The simplest call requires only three column names: estimate, lower, and upper. Here we display adjusted regression coefficients from a linear model.

dat <- data.frame(
  predictor = c("Age (per 10 y)", "Female sex", "BMI (per 5 kg/m²)",
                "Current smoker", "Physical activity"),
  estimate  = c( 0.18, -0.42,  0.11, -0.31,  0.24),
  lower     = c( 0.05, -0.61, -0.04, -0.52,  0.08),
  upper     = c( 0.31, -0.23,  0.26, -0.10,  0.40)
)

forrest(
  dat,
  estimate = "estimate",
  lower    = "lower",
  upper    = "upper",
  label    = "predictor",
  xlab     = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Group and subgroup headings
Rows whose estimate is NA become section headings. A non-empty label is rendered in bold as a group header; an empty label creates a blank spacer line. This makes it straightforward to organise results from multiple models or exposure categories.

multi_dat <- data.frame(
  label    = c(
    "Model 1 — unadjusted",
    "  Age (per 10 y)", "  Female sex", "  BMI (per 5 kg/m²)",
    "",
    "Model 2 — age and sex adjusted",
    "  Age (per 10 y)", "  Female sex", "  BMI (per 5 kg/m²)",
    "",
    "Model 3 — fully adjusted",
    "  Age (per 10 y)", "  Female sex", "  BMI (per 5 kg/m²)"
  ),
  estimate = c(
    NA,  0.21, -0.48,  0.14,
    NA,
    NA,  0.19, -0.44,  0.12,
    NA,
    NA,  0.18, -0.42,  0.11
  ),
  lower    = c(
    NA,  0.08, -0.68, -0.01,
    NA,
    NA,  0.06, -0.63, -0.03,
    NA,
    NA,  0.05, -0.61, -0.04
  ),
  upper    = c(
    NA,  0.34, -0.28,  0.29,
    NA,
    NA,  0.32, -0.25,  0.27,
    NA,
    NA,  0.31, -0.23,  0.26
  ),
  is_sum   = rep(FALSE, 14)
)

forrest(
  multi_dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  xlab       = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Adding a summary (pooled) estimate
Mark one or more rows with is_summary = TRUE to draw them as filled diamonds instead of squares. This is useful for pooled estimates in meta-analyses or for overall effects after subgroup rows.

sex_dat <- data.frame(
  label    = c("Female", "Male", "Overall"),
  estimate = c(-0.42, -0.29, -0.36),
  lower    = c(-0.61, -0.48, -0.50),
  upper    = c(-0.23, -0.10, -0.22),
  is_sum   = c(FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

forrest(
  sex_dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  xlab       = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)",
  title      = "Association of female sex with outcome by subgroup"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Log scale for ratio measures
When plotting odds ratios, hazard ratios, or risk ratios, set log_scale = TRUE and ref_line = 1.

or_dat <- data.frame(
  study    = c("Adams 2016", "Baker 2018", "Chen 2019",
               "Davis 2020", "Evans 2021", "Pooled"),
  estimate = c(1.45, 0.88, 1.21, 1.07, 0.95, 1.09),
  lower    = c(1.10, 0.65, 0.92, 0.80, 0.72, 0.94),
  upper    = c(1.91, 1.19, 1.59, 1.43, 1.25, 1.26),
  is_sum   = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

forrest(
  or_dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "study",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  log_scale  = TRUE,
  ref_line   = 1,
  xlab       = "Odds ratio (95% CI)"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Group colouring
Pass a group column to colour estimates by a categorical variable. A legend is added automatically using the Okabe-Ito colorblind-safe palette.

grp_dat <- data.frame(
  study    = c("Smith 2018", "Jones 2019", "Brown 2020",
               "Garcia 2021", "Lee 2022", "Kim 2023"),
  estimate = c(-0.18,  0.05, -0.12,  0.22, -0.08,  0.15),
  lower    = c(-0.38, -0.12, -0.30,  0.04, -0.24, -0.01),
  upper    = c( 0.02,  0.22,  0.06,  0.40,  0.08,  0.31),
  region   = c("Europe", "Europe", "Europe",
               "Americas", "Americas", "Americas")
)

forrest(
  grp_dat,
  estimate = "estimate",
  lower    = "lower",
  upper    = "upper",
  label    = "study",
  group    = "region",
  xlab     = "Mean difference (95% CI)"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Adding text columns
Use the cols argument — a named character vector mapping display headers to column names in data — to show formatted statistics alongside the plot. This triggers a multi-panel layout with the label panel on the left and text columns on the right.

or_dat$or_text <- c(
  "1.45 (1.10, 1.91)",
  "0.88 (0.65, 1.19)",
  "1.21 (0.92, 1.59)",
  "1.07 (0.80, 1.43)",
  "0.95 (0.72, 1.25)",
  "1.09 (0.94, 1.26)"
)
or_dat$pval <- c("0.009", "0.372", "0.175", "0.647", "0.676", "<0.001")

forrest(
  or_dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "study",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  log_scale  = TRUE,
  ref_line   = 1,
  header     = "Study",
  cols       = c("OR (95% CI)" = "or_text", "P-value" = "pval"),
  widths     = c(2, 4, 2, 1.2)
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Alternating row stripes
Set stripe = TRUE for a subtle alternating row background that aids readability, especially with many rows.

stripe_dat <- data.frame(
  label    = paste("Predictor", LETTERS[1:8]),
  estimate = c( 0.42, -0.18,  0.31, -0.07,  0.25, -0.12,  0.19, -0.34),
  lower    = c( 0.22, -0.38,  0.12, -0.24,  0.06, -0.30,  0.01, -0.52),
  upper    = c( 0.62,  0.02,  0.50,  0.10,  0.44,  0.06,  0.37, -0.16)
)

forrest(
  stripe_dat,
  estimate = "estimate",
  lower    = "lower",
  upper    = "upper",
  label    = "label",
  stripe   = TRUE,
  xlab     = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Study weights
Pass weight as a column name of numeric weights. Point size scales proportionally to sqrt(weight / max(weight)), so rows with larger weights appear with a bigger marker.

wt_dat <- data.frame(
  study    = paste("Study", LETTERS[1:7]),
  estimate = c(-0.20,  0.04, -0.09, -0.15,  0.03, -0.08, -0.07),
  lower    = c(-0.38, -0.10, -0.24, -0.32, -0.11, -0.22, -0.16),
  upper    = c(-0.02,  0.18,  0.06,  0.02,  0.17,  0.06,  0.02),
  weight   = c(12.1, 4.5, 9.8, 7.2, 5.3, 11.0, 50.1),
  is_sum   = c(rep(FALSE, 6), TRUE)
)

forrest(
  wt_dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "study",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  weight     = "weight",
  xlab       = "Mean difference (95% CI)"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Saving plots
Use save_forrest() to export a plot to PDF, PNG, SVG, or TIFF. Pass a zero-argument function that calls forrest().

save_forrest(
  file   = "my_forest_plot.pdf",
  plot   = function() {
    forrest(
      dat,
      estimate = "estimate",
      lower    = "lower",
      upper    = "upper",
      label    = "predictor",
      xlab     = "Regression coefficient (95% CI)"
    )
  },
  width  = 8,
  height = 5
)
